steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 1 presents the traditional view: birdsong is a male trait used to attract mates and claim territory. Text 2 presents Odom et al.'s response: the historical mischaracterization of birdsong as male is largely due to research bias — most studies were conducted near universities in the temperate northern hemisphere, where female birdsong is rarer than in the tropics. The correct answer captures exactly what Odom et al. argue caused the mistaken view in Text 1.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — They would claim that other factors than mate attraction and territorial defense have driven the evolution of singing in male birds.: Odom et al. never address why male birds sing or what drives that behavior. Their argument is about geographic sampling bias, not evolutionary drivers. ✗
  • Choice B — They would underscore that male songbirds in temperate zones are likely using their songs for different purposes than are male songbirds in the tropics.: Odom et al. contrast where research was conducted, not the purpose of male song across regions. ✗
  • Choice C — They would argue that it was influenced by the kinds of study sites researchers tended to select.: Directly reflects Odom et al.'s point — research near temperate universities skewed the findings because female birdsong is less common in those locations. The bias is geographic study-site selection. ✓
  • Choice D — They would suggest that it reflects a tendency to study male birds rather than female birds.: Odom et al. attribute the bias to where research happened, not to researchers deliberately choosing to study males over females. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
